entry.title: Re: Datasphere - Last Logon Time
entry.content:
I think that In HANA standar tables you will find only HANA DB Users (like created for Open SQL), but not the  DSP Users (they don't access HANA with their own HANA DB user)

As far as I know, the DSP task control views and the space audit are the only published data of DSP but nothing that is user-only.

Maybe in the audit the login appears (but I think not), although activating the audit of a space only for this is perhaps too much.

Regards,

entry.title: Announcing Global Availability of the Content Generation Assistant in SAP Automation Pilot!
entry.content:
Announcing General Availability of the Content Generation Assistant in SAP Automation Pilot!

We’re excited to announce that our Generative AI (GenAI) feature in SAP Automation Pilot has officially reached General Availability globally as of 20th September! 

This is a major milestone for our team and a significant step toward delivering cutting-edge functionality to our customers. It has been a long journey filled with challenges, but we’ve made it - and the future of automation looks brighter than ever.

What's New for the Content Generation Assistant?

With the latest updates, the Content Generation Assistant now offers the ability to generate scheduled executions within SAP Automation Pilot with just a single prompt. The assistant automatically creates ready-to-use executions that are scheduled to run at specific times, streamlining your workflow and reducing manual effort. This new feature adds more flexibility and control, making operations more efficient and easier to manage.

Enabling Content Generation Assistant

Activate the feature for AI-based generation of commands, executors, inputs, and scheduled executions.

Prerequisites

To enable the Generative AI capabilities, you need to have the GenAI role in your subaccount. For more information, see Permissions and Roles.

Procedure

Follow the steps below to enable or disable the AI-based generation of commands, executors, inputs, and scheduled executions.

1. From the sidebar navigation, choose Terms and Conditions.

2. Enable the Content Generation Assistant by switching on the toggle button.

3. Read and understand the terms and conditions for the usage of AI-based feature. Select the I have read and understood the terms and conditions checkbox at the bottom of the pop-up to provide your consent.

4. Choose Accept.

Result

The Content Generation Assistant is now enabled and you can generate AI-based commands, inputs, and scheduled executions. 

Try It Now!

We invite all our customers to experience the power of the Content Generation Assistant firsthand. Activate it in your SAP Automation Pilot today and start generating automation flows with ease. Whether you're looking to streamline operations, improve efficiency, or expand your automation capabilities, this tool is designed to make it happen.

To see GenAI in action, check out our How-to blog post, which guides you through setting up and using the Content Generation Assistant effectively. Additionally, in a recent success story, Sierra Digital demonstrates how they utilized the GenAI capabilities of SAP Automation Pilot to streamline SAP ECC to S/4HANA migrations, enhancing code compatibility assessments and clean core conversions.

Conclusion

The release of the Content Generation Assistant marks just the beginning of our efforts to incorporate GenAI into SAP Automation Pilot. By making automation smarter and more intuitive, we’re enabling customers to achieve greater efficiency and agility in their operations. We’re eager to see how this capability transforms the way our customers approach automation, and we look forward to continuing to innovate in this space.

Stay tuned for more updates as we push the boundaries of automation with GenAI!

entry.title: Re: Rental of equipments
entry.content:
Greetings,

you can define this by having several maintenance plants assigned to one maintancce planning plant. Your equipment would be part of the plant and could migrate between plants with relocation. If you want to change maintenance plant you first need to uninstall equipment from the functional location and then change maintancce plant (and possibly additional plant relevant data).

You can simplify this scenario if you do not require multiple maintenance plants - you can have functional locations which would represent plants and you can have these equipment migrating between them.

Best regards, Matjaž

entry.title: Preliminary What’s New Information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408.2
entry.content:
Preliminary versions of What’s New information and of the Product Assistance are now available for customers and partners to get a preview on the upcoming new and changed features of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2408.2.

You can find this preview on the current Help Portal page SAP S/4HANA Cloud (see What’s New section “Preview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408.2”). To access the Preliminary What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer or the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool.

The RASD tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The preliminary information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 2408.2 will be available in RASD within the next few days. We recommend using RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you. A valid S-User ID is needed to use the RASD tool and to view all the preliminary content stated above.

In this release, try the new Commenting Feature for What's New documentation. See this blog for more information.

entry.title: Re: How can I print a check after issuing it using the 'P...
entry.content:
Hi @ahsanali,

There is no way you could print a check using the Manage Outgoing Checks app. To be able to print a check, you may create a payment using the Create Single Payment app or Manage Automatic Payments app. Check will be printed automatically. See scope item J60 Account Payable, for setting up the printing of checks and assigning of check template.

entry.title: Re: SAP EWM RF with Screen Personas - Viewpoint functionality
Re: Returning Excel with a CAP service
entry.content:
You could try it the following way assuming the excel is in your project located:

 

const data = readFileSync(path.join(__dirname, "../files/template.xlsx"));
            
const template = new XlsxTemplate(data);
    
let sheetNumber = 1;
const values = {
      data: templateData
};
template.substitute(sheetNumber, values);

template.substitute(sheetNumber, inputParameters);

const binaryData = template.generate({type: "uint8array"});
const readableInstanceStream = new Readable({
      read() {
              this.push(binaryData);
              this.push(null);
      }
});

req._.res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet');
req._.res.setHeader('Content-Disposition', 'attachment; filename=excel.xlsx');
            
return {
        value: readableInstanceStream
};

 

 

(this example uses templating using the xslx-template lib)

entry.title: Transform Data with Javascript in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
Introduction :

  • Allows users to write executable code in JavaScript, leveraging pro-code capabilities for fusion development of enterprise automation scenarios.
  • Enables the execution of complex data type transformations and additional operations on the process context.
  • Previously it was limited to automation, now offers access to the entire context, including process inputs and custom variables, facilitating advanced data manipulation and transformation.
  • This activity comes with a new Script Editor which gives perfect IDE experience for user

Script Task Editor Features

  • Microsoft's Monaco based JS Code Editor
  • IntelliSense Support
  • Syntax highlighting
  • Type awareness
  • Console Panel- Collapsable & Expandable
  • Error Navigation
  • Other Editor Features:
    • Cut, copy, paste
    • Find all references
    • Go to definition
    • Code refactoring

Process Builder and Process Runtime  Restrictions

  • Allow till ES 5.1 standards
  • Restrict 'eval' and 'Function' constructor
  • Restrict direct manipulation of $.context, $.info, $.usertasks, allows only read
  • Read-only of input variables in context
  • Write possible only for custom variables in context
  • No addition/modification/deletion of input variables in $.context
  • Max 10,000 characters

Typical Use cases of Script Task:

  1. Array Iteration
  2. String manipulation
  3. Fetch Business Approval Status

Sample Scenario :

Lets take the below project scenario and see how Script task can come in handy.

  1. Fetch list of products from a backend system like S/4HANA 
  2. Iterate through the list of products and find the total price of all the items
  3. Concatenate Price and Currency of items which are otherwise coming from backend as two different attributes
  4. Send the total price and concatenated price and currency details as well to the approver for approval
  5. Fetch the business approval status (approved or rejected)
  6. Send Email to processor who has started the process with the approval details

The highlighted steps (ArrayList Iteration Script Task and Approval Status Script Task) are the steps pertaining to Script Task which will help with transforming the data , to find the total price of all items and concatenate price and currency (points 2 and 3 above).

Lets see in detail how the Script Tasks in this sample scenario has been created below :

  1. Custom variables are created to write the manipulated data and use it across the process 
  2. Process Input variables can be read but cannot be written to using Script task
  3. Process context up to previous steps can be read from the left side panel directly 
  4. When the context variables including Process inputs or Custom variables are chosen from the left side panel, automatically java script code for that gets populated in the right side of the editor where the code is actually written
  5. Script Editor comes with some sample code snippets to enable CzD also to start coding in java script.
  6. Intellisense support will help the user with recommendations as he types

Array Iteration Script Task:

  1. The items received from the previous Actions API call can be iterated through and the price of each item is summed up and total price is calculated 
  2. In the same iteration , the attribute values of price and currency are concatenated so that it can be sent across to the next step in the process which is the approval form

 

Fetch Approval Status Script Task:

  1. Business Approval status of whether the approval task has been approved or rejected can  be determined with this Script task
  2. $.usertasks ,$.info and $.roles allow access to identifiers of the current task or the exact execution.For more information on this, you can refer here
  3. We will make use of $.usertasks to fetch the business approval status
  4. $.usertasks has to be followed by the approval formId which can be obtained from intellisense or by clicking on one of the attributes of the approval form from left panel and replacing $.context with $.usertasks

 Isolated Code Execution - Testing

  • Easy to test written code even without process deployment.
  • Users can provide sample input values for the context variables used in the script.
  • Upon clicking the "Run Test" button, the code will be executed in the browser's JavaScript engine, and the result will be displayed instantly. 

 You can refer to the YouTube video on the same topic here for more details. 

Whats coming up - Further Enhancements :

  1. Generating the javascript code using Generative AI capability
  2. Reuse of Scripts

Happy Scripting and Transforming Data as per your need 😊

entry.title: SAUG Sydney Summit August 2024
entry.content:
I usually start with a picture of a dustbin, so I asked the AI to “draw me a picture of a dustbin at an SAP conference”.

SAP Dustbin #1

That is an amazing result. It has the SAP logo; it looks just like a conference. The woman has no thumbs, and I do not think her chair has a back, but oh well, otherwise well done the AI. It then suggested to me some improvements, so I told it to go ahead. Here was the result.

SAP Dustbin #2

It is not very good at spelling, but I also like the result. There is a lot of ABAP code out there (some of it standard SAP) which should be thrown in the bin.

Day One – 19/08/2024

KEYNOTES

It was mentioned that SAP – as a company – seems to be having a mid-life crisis. In a human that would be a man in their mid-forties starting to go to discos or raves, getting a tattoo, trying to get an 18-year-old girlfriend, and so on. With SAP maybe the equivalent is going all-in on the Cloud and AI.

The head of SAUG is from Heidelberg (where I lived for about five years) and she mentioned that “Artificial Intelligence” in German is “Künstliche Intelligenz” which does not sound that good in English, especially how it is pronounced.

Anyway, the next big conference in Australia is in November, it is called SAUSAGE-X and is part of the “SAP TECHED On Tour” program. For many years there were three TECHED in-person events – in Las Vegas, in Barcelona and in Bangalore. Then came COVID and all events went virtual. After COVID ended SAP only had one TECHED – in Bangalore – so the American SAP User Group held their own version in New Orleans. I went there, spoke at the event, it was GREAT, ten times better than a “real” TECHED. New Orleans is a wonderful city also.

This year we have the concept of “SAP TECHED on Tour”. That is instead of three in-person events there are assorted in-person events in many different places, all co-located with the local SAP User Group conference.

As I said I live in Australia, so we have a SAUSAGE event in Melbourne from the 19th-20th of November. The original plan was that the 19th would the SAUSAGE conference and the 20th would be the SAP TECHED conference, but the current plan is to sort of merge them, as I understand it. Some people have said that a conference that was formerly free, and now costs 1600 dollars plus you get marketing shoved down your throat that did not happen before, is not good value. I do not know what they mean. I have been approved to speak at the event, about AI in ABAP. If one does not put a buzzword like “AI” in ones abstract, no way will it get approved.

Jo Wagstaff – Leadership / Update Your Inner Operating System

She offered everyone in the room a free one-hour leadership consultation which was nice. I do not ever want to be a manager as you have to stab competent people in the back in order to jump over them, and sack useless people, and I do not want to do either, I think there is something else managers do, but I cannot remember what it is.

She mentioned we are living in a VUCA world. I had no idea what that meant, I thought we were living in a Barbie World. More on VUCA later.

GAI-SHI NGUYEN – SAP SIGNAVIO

Just to start off with Signavio is a sort of “process mining” company. I will go into more detail shortly, but I know the general concept as I first encountered it in 1997 when my company first went down the SAP road. The idea is that moving onto SAP is not just an IT replacement, it is a “Business Transformation” project. That us, it is pointless doing the wrong thing you have been doing for twenty years faster, you need to look at can you change that wrong thing into the right thing. And I can tell you people HATE that idea. Henry Ford said, “If I had asked what people wanted, they would have told me they wanted faster horses”. So, he went down a different path.

I have been bouncing around Australia like a lunatic recently. My original plan was that I would be in the Gold Coast (Queensland) for the SAP Signavio Event. I even went into the hotel (Mantra) to see what the bar was like, and they gave me a free mulled wine. That was great. Sadly, I had to go back to Sydney, so I will not be going to that event. And then my global company said they had decided not to use Signavio anyway, they picked CELONIS SYMBIO. SAP sales had been pushing Celonis to their customers for years anyway, before SAP bought Signavio, so that should not come as a gigantic shock.

It transpires that while Signavio was an independent company, SAP was their largest customer. So, SAP was using Signavio for “process mining” and selling Celonis to their customers to solve the same problem, because they (SAP) had a contract to do so. This sort of thing happens in business all the time, you just do not know what is going to happen in ten minutes time.

SAP bought Signavio three years ago. That product looks at HOW business processes work.   Then one year ago SAP bought LEANIX which is more to do with hardware i.e., WHERE the business process happens behind the scenes, IT architecture if you will, and the two things mesh seamlessly, and then the marketing people forced the presenter to mention SAP had bought WALKME (which does something or other) as well so he did, but as much as marketing wants that new acquisition to fit into the big picture they are not quite there yet, Which is not to say that WALKME is not a good product, I am sure it is, but forcing something new on a speaker, whilst they are speaking, is a bit like the novel 1984.

Signavio was described as “Taking an intellectual view of transformation”. I am sorry to say I have no idea what that means, maybe I am not as intellectual as I thought I was and need to transform myself.

Another concept discussed was that moving targets are not bad (as this always happens) but moving the baseline is a bad thing. I can relate to the latter – I was once on a project (not going to say where) where the bonuses where dependent on each phase of the “waterfall” being complete on a certain date. None of them ever were but the target dates were adjusted retrospectively to match the actual completion dates, so all the bonuses got paid. Good news for me, but that is possibly not how things should be done.

A good quote from this speech was “Content is King / Context is King Kong”. That makes sense to me. Information is no good if it does fit into the Jigsaw Puzzle, so to speak. As an example, do my rambling, incoherent observations help form an overall picture as to what the presentation was about?

The last point I noted was about how complexity is not a bad thing in and of itself – if you can master it. That makes me think of an observation Lars Peterson made about the “clean core” which was a room can be clean even if there are lots of things inside that room. Removing useful things from that clean room does not make the room any cleaner.

COLES – RISE WITH SAP – CAMERON AMLING

RISE with SAP

Coles is a large Australian supermarket chain. Apart from the SAP core they use Ariba, Successfactors and Fieldglass. I am a shareholder in Coles and of course SAP.

The RISE program is all about migrating to S/4HANA. I had in my notes the person from Coles said this meant that RISE took over some tasks that were formerly done internally and this resulted in more cost and less flexibility. I may have got that wrong, but that is what I wrote done. That cannot be true, surely?

The next quote I had written down was “There is no real payback in this (moving to S/4HANA), the whole thing is just a cost of staying in business, cost avoidance”. That does not sound too positive either.

To tie things together (to the last presentation) Coles used Signavio.

They also attempted to do the ever-popular “Clean Core” approach to all their custom code during the migration to S/4HANA. The quote was (and I am sure of this one) “The benefits of this are huge. We have not seen any yet, but we will eventually”. I tend to think they will. I hope they will.

They are using Datasphere instead of BW even though many at SAP have said to me from day one that Datasphere was not a replacement for BW. The difference was that BW extracted and replicated data into a central place whereas with Datasphere the data stayed in the source systems.

SMARTSHIFT

Acting Ensign Wesley Crusher – Woolworths

Ardnt Hoffmann – Smartshift

Woolworths means different things in different countries. There used to be one in Heidelberg near the Galeria Kaufhof. In Australia it is a big supermarket chain. I am also a shareholder of that AUS company. Talk about putting your money where your mouth is – I am a shareholder of my own company (which runs SAP), and SAP itself, and multiple companies which also run SAP. So, it could be said I have a vested interest in SAP doing well. Thus, if I say anything which sounds negative about SAP it is with the intention of “constructive criticism”.

Woolworths uses SuccessFactors + Ariba + SAP BTP. All well and good, where I work, we have Ariba, and we chose Workday over SuccessFactors for whatever reason.

The pertinent thing is my organisation has chosen Smartshift for the code remediation, so as might be imagined we are very interested how previous companies have gone with this.

Woolworths said they are trying to keep the core “clean-ish” which to be honest is realistically as good as you are going to get. My notes said they are aiming for an S/4HANA go-live in 2026.

Smartshift described themselves as a “washing machine” for custom ABAP code, an ABAP Laundromat if you will (in-joke). On a washing machine there are multiple options you can choose when cleaning your clothes. In this case most customers (of Smartshift) choose the “full wash” option.

In any event, the first step is a gigantic bunch of rules which get applied to your Z code to change it automatically to make it compliant with S/4HANA. That also includes an unused code analysis (which you could do yourself if you wanted with standard SAP tools) and a check for hard coded literals (my pet hate).

Afterwards you could optionally start a second wash cycle called “Clean Core X-Ray” with the idea of making your code cloud ready.

The results – in this case specifically but apparently quite generally – is that only 10% of the automated fixes are to do with making the code ready for S/4HANA. The rest are to do with modernisation/optimisation. I could have told you that, more on this shortly.

In the last session there were quotes that were not 100% positive, here comes another one from this session; “This does not save any time or money, the benefit is allowing our team to focus on other things”. That is a bit of a mixed compliment, but honesty is the best policy.

I would like to end my review of this session by making three points, all my own observations, nothing to do with anything mentioned at the conference.

  • If every time you make a code change you do the SLIN/SCI exercise, then when the time comes to go to S/4HANA you will be laughing. People are generally too scared to do this, I am lucky enough to work in a company where such things are allowed.
  • Once your development systems is in an S/4HANA environment, in the “ABAP Development Tools” often known as “ABAP in Eclipse” if you run the ATC (ABAP Test Cockpit) checks on all of your Z code base at once, then the tool will propose a load of automated fixes to make the code S/4HANA compliant and you just press a button and they will all be applied automatically.
  • What scares me the most about Smartshift is it seems to insert a load of comments around the code it fixes, and that could lead to a program ending up with 95% comments surrounding tiny islands of code. I would then spend the next twenty years deleting those comments as after about a week those comments have no value at all, and make the code more difficult to understand/maintain. I am told you can turn that auto-comment thing off. I hope so.

Then it was ME! ME ME ME ME!

To secure a speaking spot at an SAP event often you have to use a “buzzword”. AI will get you in no questions asked. I decided to use the word “sustainability” which is also a hot topic.

I have worked in the building materials industry for 34 years now, and we are a big carbon dioxide emitter. However, we are moving from being part of the problem to being part of the solution.

  • It has been said that a genius is someone how spots the obvious first. A fine example is “wheels on luggage”. The concept was not invented until after humanity had landed people on the moon. In retrospect the idea is obvious. Amazingly this idea was not perfected (four wheels instead of two) till 1991.
  • In a similar way much more recently, Elon Musk observed that if you had to build an entire new aircraft every time someone wanted to fly from London to New York then there would not be very many flights. Yet that was the way space travel had always been, build a new rocket each time. The solution was to have a rocket and try and make it 100% re-usable. You might not get to 100% but at least try.
  • Traditionally once a building gets demolished, the bulk of the building materials get “thrown away” as it were – buried in landfill or some such. Moreover, once the components of concrete have been bonded together, they cannot be unbound – you cannot unscramble the egg. You cannot get the cement back out. Or so everyone thought – until now.
  • Thus, in 2018 Hanson Australia acquired the Alex Fraser Group which specialises in recycled building materials. This was just the first step – such acquisitions are happening in our organisation all around the world. The idea is that every year we use more recycled building materials, thus needing less new cement and whatever, moving from using 100% new materials each time, to the maximum recycled percentage we can realistically achieve.

Clean Core – South Australia Power Network

Odirile Malongwa / Chandan Singh

Like the vast bulk of organisations, the South Australian Power Network has had 25 years of “Z” development. I know that feeling well.

If we are going to have a plug, this company uses RevTrac for change control, and so does my company. You could do a lot worse.

They (SAPN) have been told by SAP – as have we all – that Z development is a bad thing, and now everybody must have a “clean core”. The logic being that the “cleaner the core” the easier it is to do an upgrade. If I was in a cynical mood, I would say that most companies never use anything new from an upgrade anyway, they do a “technical upgrade” and say they are going to look at all the new functionality later but never do. But ANYWAY, you have to do upgrades whether you like it or not, so might as well make it as easy as possible.

They developed a new decision table for new developments. If an application was tightly coupled to SAP data – for example a Z table that was tied to VBAK – then this should live “embedded” within the SAP ERP system, connected via stable APIS. If it was totally Z data not related to anything than CAP/BTP was the way to go. Unless my memory is totally wrong in the questions, they said thus far every new thing has been embedded.

One very new concept that SAP has come up with is the idea of “stability contracts”. The idea is not new – that is what BAPIS were for, and they are about 20 years old – but I am very interested in what the proposal is this time.

SAPN (quite funny that the first three letters are SAP) had some “gotchas” which is after all, why we come to these conferences – to hear the “war stories”.

  • Don’t use implicit enhancements (I was very pleased when SAP came out with this idea, it was really useful, but even then, I think it was over ten years ago, I could see trouble ahead).
  • When extending SAP apps you can create a custom business object. All well and good, but the object is created using BOPF, and that is obsolete, so do not do that.
  • Adding fields to the structure CI_QMEL (something to do with plant maintenance) to a Fiori app causes data loss, so do not do that either.

QUIZ

Many people say technology is useless, and often they are correct. However nowadays you can point your phone at a huge QR code on the stage, and then take part in an interactive quiz all about SAP and IT topics, which is really funny, and you can win a ticket to TECHED in Melbourne, which at 1600 dollars is not to be sneezed at.

CLOSING KEYNOTE – 20 YEARS A CONSULTANT

Rukshana Sameem – FI/CO Consultant

This lady has worked in multiple countries for many organisations over 20 years. That trumps me, although I have been working with SAP since 1997, and have worked in multiple countries it has all been for the same company.

She shared the successes and failures over that period. Naturally successes are good, but people are more interested in the failures, out of morbid curiosity I read somewhere that 70%+ of major IT projects fail, not just SAP, but all across the board.

The most interesting failure was when a UK subsidiary had to go live in a hurry, and “they” decided just to copy across the USA credit control area customising settings to a new UK credit control area and not test it. What could possibly go wrong? As it transpires “they” forgot to change the credit control area currency from USD to GBP, and of course all the other customising settings were in GBP, and the standard SAP system cannot handle this situation at all, so things went pear-shaped very quickly indeed on go-live day.

Her most important lesson was that when presented with a requirement do not instantly go into solution mode, but rather step back and think – make sure you know what you are really being asked. I know this as a programmer. Often the functional requirement is written by someone who does not know what they want, or who they are, or what year is this, or who is the President ARRRRGGGHHH! And then they leap off the ledge, wearing a spacesuit. If you have worked in IT for any length of time surely you must be familiar with this phenomenon.

DEMO JAM BTP

DEMO Jam BTP

At time of writing, I am willing to bet many SAP customers do not even know what BTP stands for, let alone what it’s purpose in life is. That is a bit of a shame as it could be said this is the future of SAP – or at least that is the current plan.

So, to answer the question “What is this weird thing and what is it FOR?” the SAUSAGE organisation invited multiple companies to build imaginative use cases for the BTP product and then the best four would compete live on stage to win the grand prize of half a bunch of bananas and a tin of sardines and a one-way trip to Mars.

Each team had four minutes to present, and just like the quiz earlier the audience could vote on the spot the instant the contest had finished via QR code on the big screen.

Just as background BTP consists of a bug bunch of “services” which you can subscribe to. Each comes at a cost, so you just choose the ones you feel you really need. That is a bit of a radical mindset change. It is like going to the supermarket and just buying what you want as and when you want it, rather than having to pay seventeen million dollars each year and getting a years’ worth of everything in the supermarket all at once, and then not using 95% of the goods.

Here are the four contestants. I will describe them in the order I voted for them (that is, the best one last, best one being totally subjective, though it was the one that won).

Fujitsu – Vendor Management + Payment System. If some people were going to be snide and nasty, they would ask is that not what Ariba is for? Or master data management? Or indeed the core ERP system itself? Anyway, Fujitsu built such a beast in “The Cloud” using BTP.

Deloitte – Service Entry Sheets – these are for when you want to pay someone for a service (hence the name) and they are a standard SAP object, but they are not much fun to use in the standard SAP system. Hence, any sort of application to make this process easier is always welcome.

SA Power Networks – Plant Maintenance – here they used AI to analyse both the text in work order notifications (what people write into SAP when a machine breaks down) and entire technical manuals. Thus, when someone asks a question like “are any machines currently dangerous (to human life) and if so, what do I do about it?” they get an intelligent response.

Lion Brewery – How to do a Pub Crawl – of course I am going to vote this one to be my favourite. But so did everyone else!  The AI bit was taking a photograph of all the beer pumps at any given pub and thus working out what exactly was available in which venue. Turning unstructured data into structured data. Is that really AI or is it OCR such as I talked about at a conference in 2007? You can be the judge.

Food Review (photo generated by AI)

The photo below  is exactly how the evening event was. A guy with three arms, two holding a beer, his third arm had no hand yet was still feeding him a burger. Another guy drinking three beers at once, one of which was floating in mid-air. What was that about AI taking all our jobs any day now?

Food Review

DAY TWO! 20/08/2004

KEYNOTES!

Professor Michaell Roseman – AI in the Future

I suppose there is no getting away from AI. There was a lot less of it in this conference than in previous ones.

Apparently, we are in the “third wave” of digital transformation. Some would say that digital watches were the start of digital transformation but let us not go there. The three waves are: -

  1. Internal Procedures
  2. Customer Led Procedures
  3. Data-Led Procedures, empowered by AI

Sting once said “Love is the Seventh Wave” so we will get there eventually.

The question was raised – can AI do something truly new, as opposed to just a productivity gain, which is what we have always used IT for to date? That makes sense to me, when a lot of people a talk to say what they want from AI it is just automation of existing processes, and there is nothing new about that.

He said that literacy is now more important than numeracy – that is people do not know how to search (phrase their query). That is a problem in Google search and is a much bigger problem in the good old AI co-pilot. A real human could guess what it was you were asking for / looking for but these AI things have no intelligence at all (my words not his) so they cannot interpret what you say unless you are really precise.

TCODES made users have to think like the SAP system. The “new” programming language is English (I think someone said that back in 1950 but now maybe we are getting there. Maybe. Hundredth time lucky).

The next idea was the concept of the “reverse prompt”. That is, currently when you are talking to one of those useless “digital assistant” things on a website you ask questions and get an irrelevant response. In the future the idea is that the AI assistant will start asking you questions, in the same way if you were having an online chat with a real human agent, they would ask you questions. I first encountered that concept in the computer game “The Hobbit” for the ZX Spectrum round about 1984. The reverse prompts were not very clever back then all they said was “hurry up”. I did manage to get this working on my BBC Microcomputer the next month.

The next point was that the idea of AI is that you should end up trusting AI to know what you want more than you trust yourself. The example given was someone with dementia. Now, my short-term memory has been terrible all my life, but I am not sure I trust the current iterations of AI at all in any way shape or form to come up with anything halfway sensible.

Every time we have a technological revolution everyone gets worried that they are going to lose their jobs. The position here was that AI creates jobs rather than removing them. I cannot recall exactly why, but the phrase “Digital Minions” was coined, and they need to be constantly watched otherwise they go all crackers just like the real Minions.

Sven Denecken – AI Right Now – SAP AI / BTP / RISE

Here are the bullet points I wrote down.

  • What is the world is “Taulia”. I looked it up and it is a” working capital management” company SAP bought a few years back. I am guessing that company is a heavy user of AI in some form.
  • RISE with SAP is for big companies, using the private cloud. GROW is for small companies, using the public cloud.
  • A statement was made “SE80 is for customising”. NO IT IS NOT! SPRO is for customising, SE80 is for custom development. Customising transports (client dependant) and development transports (client-independent) are totally disparate things but if even top people at SAP do not understand the difference then I am in despair.
  • This next one is just my observation that the “clean core” thing being mooted now is 100% the same as what was being mooted when I started my first SAP project in 1997. Back then everything tried (and failed) to follow that advice, and I don’t like everyone’s chances this time around either.
  • 80% of the entire world’s GDP is generated is some sense via an SAP system. And yet no-one I meet, even at international airports, has ever heard of SAP. The phrase is “the biggest company nobody has ever heard of”.
  • Despite the official position that “Datasphere” is not a replacement for BW, many presentations I hear take the contrary position. Here it was said that BW is sub-optimal because the ETL process that moves the data from the ERP system to BW becomes out of date one second after it arrives. Whereas Datasphere works with the live data in the source systems.

Tea Break

At this point the monsters started coming out of the walls to eat the delegates. I always thought that might happen. It was only ever a matter of time, really. Still, most people ran away quickly and so survived in order to be able to attend the next keynote.

Tea Break

Indiana Jones and the Care Home of Destiny – Dr. Nicholas Nicoloudis

It was reported on Linked in that I cut my own head off with an axe, so the next keynote speaker could use my head as a prop. That was not 100% true. In fact, I have a hat a bit like the one Harrison Ford used in the movies, so the speaker asked to borrow that hat for his speech. I was happy to oblige.

As you know, in the last Indiana Jones film, the actor was 127 years old, so all the action took place in an old people’s home that got raided by time travelling Nazis from 1944 trying to invade the future (see “Lightning” by Dean R. Koontz for further details) and the hero beat them all to death with his Zimmer frame.

To some people that plot seemed a trifle far-fetched, so in this presentation the plot was a few percent more believable. In this plot he hero rushes round Skull Island in a desperate attempt to find that AI s useful for businesses, facing huge obstacles at every turn.

In the exciting race around the island Indicia Jones discovered the following.

  • The Joule licensing model is so complicated no human could ever understand it. You would probably need an AI to help you. But for that you would a licence.
  • This next one I 100% understand. In Germany you have something called a “works council” which is sort of like a union but embedded within the company. The idea is that it protects the rights of workers. I worked in Germany for a long time, so I know how amazingly powerful the works council is. In the example given, there were microphones being used by an AI to see if machines (in a factory) were in trouble or not, but the works council said those microphones could be spying on the employees, so that was the end of that.
  • On the flipside, at Samsung it was reported employees were telling CHATGPT secret things, which then went all over the internet. Hence, the need for company specific AI systems, like the one I am using to produce the pictures in this blog.
  • 47% of employees (everywhere) say that they have no idea at all how co-pilot can help them. I have seen that. Everyone gets an email saying you now have access to co-pilot, and your life will be so much better now, and what is really need is some sort of guidance, any sort of guidance, on what to use it for, but that would take time/money, so it does not happen.
  • 77% of those surveyed said that AI tools make things worse than before. Just to re-iterate it is someone from SAP presenting these statistics.
  • The “BUILD” code generation (done by an AI) does not work properly as yet, you always need a developer to fix it. Which is fine by me, as I am a developer. As an aside, I am coming over all negative here, but these AI things are getting better every day, and the day will dawn they will be as useful as all the hype starting in 1950 said. Hopefully I will still be alive to see it.
  • The idea is to be able to “chat with your data”. I liked that one. The concept is crystal clear. If and when that works, it would be the bees knees.
  • Lastly there was a screenshot from PWC and that was all about an AI helping a company buying concrete from the very company I work for. I liked that as well, as might be imagined.

That was a wonderful speech, in lots of ways, especially in terms of being entertaining to the audience, he would have scored a load of points at Toastmasters. All presenters at IT should be as good as that, I aspire to be, but many do not make the effort, or even understand why it is important. I got my hat back as well.

At this stage in the SAP conference some of the delegates who had been bitten by monsters earlier now turned into monsters themselves. Isn’t that always the way? But if you wear a red suit, you are safe.

Red Suit Makes You Safe

AGL – Trying to be User Friendly

AGL stands for “Australian Gas and Light”. I use them for both my gas and electricity supply, I am also a shareholder (do you see a pattern forming here?) some of my work colleagues left my company to join the SAP department at AGL. Now, I could be making this up, but from what I hear at conferences AGL seem to have a bottomless, endless, pit of money to draw on for IT projects. Good for them.

In this case the point of the presentation was that the Fiori design pattern is nice, but most standard S/4HANA applications are not very nice. So, the running “Tale of Two Cities” analogy that was used throughout the conference was once again invoked,

One fun fact I learned which I had no idea about was that a “dashboard” is something on a horse drawn carriage to stop mud getting splashed on the passengers as they travel. I have no idea at all how that term mutated into the current IT meaning of the word.

As you have gathered by now 99.99% of what I am writing is my reaction to what people said at the conference rather than what people said.

After listening to this speech here is what I think. Me personally.

  • Nothing has changed since 2000.
  • Back then we did not like the standard SAP reports, so we built custom ALV reports in the SAP GUI.
  • Now, in 2024, we do not like the standard Fiori reports, so we build custom UI5 reports to run in a browser.
  • In an IT department you always have the “Build vs Buy” decision, which is cheaper/better. That decision is horribly complicated but for one application AGL decided to “buy” and that may have been the wrong decision as the 3rd party application took one year to upgrade. I have heard a fair few examples of this sort of thing.

Hydro-Tasmania – Centre of Excellence

Tasmania is a (very large) island off the south coast of Australia, and as it is relatively close to the south pole, it rains all the time. Until all the ice in the south pole melts but let us not go there.

As such, just like Scotland, Tasmania can run on hydro-electric power. Hydro-Tasmania is a government owned entity so I cannot have shares in them, but I DO have shares in the Scottish equivalent company (HA HA HA HA HA! BOAST BOAST BOAST!).

You may have heard the term “centre of excellence” or “competency centre”. The first one sounds a lot better to me. Anyway, it is a name you give to your IT department to make it sound better. Because excellence should come as standard.

One thing I agreed with 100% was the idea of having a roster of IT people going out to the “coal face” to meet the real people who use the IT systems you create. I love doing that. I do it on my holidays when I see a concrete plant.

It was quite a good speech, but at the end the speaker was eaten by monsters. I suppose that is the risk you take, speaking at SAP conferences.

The Risk You Take

Bees

This is really important. The master of ceremonies – Tim – made the observation that Bees are not German. By which he meant that most bees follow all the rules, but there are a few radical bees that break all the rules and go exploring, seeing what they can find to bring back to the hive.

I reckon the founders of SAP – all German – were just like those radical bees.

Closing Keynotes

Rheem – Enterprise AI

They have a chatbot called Rhonda. The compere asked what that reminded him of, and I gave the correct answer “Help me Rhonda”.

Is AI like a smartphone? That is a smartphone replaces alarm clocks, wallets, torches, newspapers and so on. Is AI going to make a similar seismic change? At a previous conference someone mentioned in predictions of the future people thought we would have more different things we use every day but the reality is the other way around.

Sometimes you get told a new phrase you have never heard before, and then suddenly you see and hear it everywhere. In this case the phrase – new to me – was a “wicked” problem. That is a problem that is difficult to define and does not have a single correct answer. I had never heard that in my life and the next week it was all over the Australian papers.

There was much talk about how good automatic quarterly releases were (in Cloud products) but I cannot help but think to myself that even when companies upgraded every five years they did not use most (or any) of the new features, and now they do not use most (or any) of the new features every three months so what has changed really?

Sarah Reichman – Genfluencing in the age of VUCA and AI

This was about the fact that millennials will make up 75% of the workforce by 2025. And different generations think differently, the example given was that old people like me (I am 56) think working five days a week (or every day really since I am in IT) is normal, lots of younger people think working three days a week (for the same money) is normal and if you question that you get told you are living in the stone age.

I could not help but think of a line from the TV program “Colin from Accounts” where a load of young people make fun of a guy who is about 40 saying things like “You are revealing your Inner Boomer!”. At one stage one of the young women says, “I am an influencer – I don’t pay for anything!”

I would be crazy to say that things are not different at work now than when I joined 34 years ago, but Socrates said something about young people not being as good as the previous generation, and a Roman Centurion in his diary talked about something similar. They both had the exact same problem – young people did not think much of them and their old-fashioned fossilized ideas, and they did not think much of the way the young people were behaving. So, is any of this really new? Or does it just go around in a circle as in the song line “Doing things I used to do, they think are new, I sit and watch, as time goes by”.

One interesting statistic quoted was that 97% of managers think they are doing a good job. That implies that 3% of managers think they are useless. If you thought you were hopeless at your current job why would you (a) admit it in a survey and (b) stay in the job and not try and find something else you were better at?

More AI – Gia-Th Nguyen – Signavio

I was going to go to the Signavio event in Sydney, but I ended up having to go to Brisbane the day before. Then I was going to go to the Signavio event in Brisbane, but I ended up having to go back to Sydney the day before. So, I am not as well up on this product as I might possibly be. Then my organization said we are not going to be using Signavio after all, we are going to use SYMBIO by Celonis instead, because SAP have pushed Celonis to their customers so hard for so long.

The most important fact from this presentation was that “intelligence” is Latin for “to read between the lines”.  Now, if an AI could do that, work out information that was not explicitly mentioned in whatever it was trying to analyse, wouldn’t that be good?

Surprise Appearance by senior SAP management

SAP Management

What better way to end the conference that lots of managers turning up (pictured above). I asked one about the roadmap for AI and BTP but he asked me to sniff his flower first, which of course squirted water at me. Then he said if I wanted to know the future of BTP I should look through this telescope, which I did, but it was a “black eye telescope”. Then he threw a bucket of confetti at me.

Conclusion

That was another very good conference. It was maybe not as focused as the Brisbane one earlier this year, but there was a lot less AI presentations, which can only be a good thing.

Next up is the one in Melbourne, the TECHED event. I just got the green light to speak at that; my subject being about AI as it relates to ABAP programming based on my experiments. I used to think all this AI stuff was a load of old nonsense, and vomited into a bucket whenever anyone mentioned it, but now (based on actual experiments in the last 48 hours prior to writing this) I think I am going to have to revise my position somewhat.

Cheersy Cheers

Paul

entry.title: Re: In adobe forms (SFP)- display logo, which was uploaded via SMW0.
entry.content:
Try Below Code:

DATA: lv_xstring  TYPE                   xstring,
      lv_obkey    TYPE                   wwwdatatab,
      ls_oparams  TYPE sfpoutputparams,
      lv_funcname TYPE  funcname,
      lt_mime     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF w3mime.

lv_obkey-objid = 'ZNABL_LOGO'.
lv_obkey-relid = 'MI'.

CALL FUNCTION 'WWWDATA_IMPORT'
  EXPORTING
    key               = lv_obkey
  TABLES
    mime              = lt_mime
  EXCEPTIONS
    wrong_object_type = 1
    import_error      = 2
    OTHERS            = 3.
LOOP AT lt_mime ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_mime>).
  CONCATENATE lv_xstring <ls_mime>-line INTO lv_xstring IN BYTE MODE.
ENDLOOP.

"Pass this Xstring in Adobe Form


CALL FUNCTION 'FP_JOB_OPEN'
  CHANGING
    ie_outputparams = ls_oparams " Form Processing Output Parameter
  EXCEPTIONS
    cancel          = 1               " Canceled by user
    usage_error     = 2
    system_error    = 3               " System Error
    internal_error  = 4               " Internal Error
    OTHERS          = 5.

CALL FUNCTION 'FP_FUNCTION_MODULE_NAME'
  EXPORTING
    i_name     = 'ZVIVE_TEST_FORM'              " Form
  IMPORTING
    e_funcname = lv_funcname.

CALL FUNCTION lv_funcname
  EXPORTING
    lv_logo = lv_xstring.

entry.title: Re: What is the tcode for finding the warehouse number un...
entry.content:
Hello @RaheemFMC 

Warehouse numbers are assigned to plant/storage location combination in SPRO: Enterprise Structure -> Assignment -> Logistics Execution -> Assign Warehouse Number to Plant and Storage Location. These are 3 chars, LE-WM warehouse numbers.

Then  3 chars, LE-WM warehouse numbers are assigned to 4 char EWM warehouse numbers in SPRO: Integration with Other SAP Components -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Assign Warehouse Number to EWM Warehouse Number.

Those settings are customized on ERP side, not in EWM.

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Advice on Reducing DHCDC_RTSTORE Table Size in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hello,

1. please check how many records are in DHCDC_RTSTORE
Note that the table is changing rapidly, and it could be that the table is not containing many records and therefore needs to be shrinked on DB level to free disk space - your DBA should know how to do it.

2. if there are many old records, check if there are hanging subscriptions and that your rentention periods are set properly - inactive subscriptions (including data) should be automatically deleted after the rentention.
See Note https://me.sap.com/notes/3088317/E for more information.

3. If you are unsure about the above tasks please open a ticket.

DO NOT delete the manually data using SQL (as adviced before), without explicit ask from the support team!

entry.title: Project Settlement with Parallel Ledgers (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)
entry.content:
Reasons for Parallel Accounting

Companies often operate in diverse economic environments, and they might have various stakeholders, such as investors, regulators, and management, each requiring different types of financial information. To satisfy these differing needs, companies have to support different accounting principles in parallel. Here are some examples why:

  • Different countries have different financial reporting requirements and standards. 
    A multinational company operating in multiple jurisdictions might need to prepare financial statements that comply with each region's regulatory requirements.
  • Tax authorities may have their own set of accounting rules and principles distinct from financial accounting rules.
  • Internal management needs financial information tailored to specific operational or strategic goals.
    This might involve adjusting the standard accounting principles to reflect internal performance metrics or budgeting needs.
  • Supporting different accounting principles can enhance transparency and credibility with stakeholders.
    It can demonstrate that a company is diligent in maintaining accurate and reliable financial records.

In S/4HANA we update every posting according to the rules of the different parallel accounting principles in parallel ledgers. Each ledger can have a different accounting principle assigned.
There are different reasons why posted values can differ for different accounting principles. E.g.,

  • different capitalization rules for materials in the different GAAPs
  • different depreciation keys/useful life
  • ledger-specific postings, e.g. provisions

These different values also need to be reflected in follow-on processes like e.g.,

  • CO allocations like universal allocation, overhead calculation, or settlement
  • cost rate calculation
  • WIP and variance calculation

 

Differences in Settlement with and without Parallel Ledgers

But what exactly is the difference if settlement is enabled for parallel ledger handling?

The example in the picture above shows that, if different values are posted in the different ledgers, follow-on processes like for example settlement, have to take care of these different values and calculate the values to be settled separately per ledger. 

Without parallel ledger enablement, settlement is calculating the value to be settled only for the leading ledger, the settlement value from the leading ledger will then be posted in all parallel ledger. This leads to the following issues:

  • If different values have been posted for different ledgers, the settled project will only have a balance of zero in the leading ledger. 
  • The ledger specific values get lost for follow on processes.
    The picture above shows that without parallel accounting the value of the own produced fixed asset will be 175€ in all ledgers. With parallel accounting, the value for the fixed asset is different in the different ledgers.
    This example shows that even CO postings like project settlement influence balance sheet values and external reporting. This is why all postings need to support parallel ledgers.

In the public cloud edition, parallel ledgers and ledger-specific settlement is always active.

 

Ledger-Specific Settlement/ Distribution Rules

Defining ledger-specific settlement/ distribution rules is an optional functionality. For most of the use cases, ledger-independent rules will work fine (also with parallel accounting and ledger-specific settlement active).

A possible use case for ledger-specific rules is the settlement to fixed assets. The portion of costs that can be activated for the final asset, can differ in different GAAPs. In this case there is the need to define different rules for different ledgers.

If needed, the option for ledger-specific rules has to be activated in the configuration for the settlement profile:

Even if the option for ledger-specific rules is active, it is still possible to define ledger-independent rules.
A rule definition could look like this:

The system will first use ledger-specific rules, if no ledger-specific rules are defined for a ledger, the rule with ledger <blank> will be used.

 

Settlement History  

If a period is settled for the first time, the distribution rules that are valid for this period will be copied to the settlement history for the settled ledger and if they are relevant for distributing amounts to settlement receivers, they will be marked as used in the settlement period. For used rules the fields 'First Used' and 'Last Used' will be set accordingly.

If the same period is settled again, the settlement program will first check the settlement history and if distribution rules are already available for the settlement period and the settled ledger, those will be relevant for the settlement, even if the user has changed the settlement receivers in the meantime.

That means that changing the rule is not sufficient if a settlement already happened in the settlement period. If the new settlement receiver shall be taken into account for a settlement in the same period, the entry in the settlement history for the settlement period with the old receiver needs to first be removed by reversing all settlements that happened in the settlement period for the ledger. The last reversal will then either limit the validity of the distribution rule so that it is not valid for the settlement period or delete the entry in the settlement history completely, depending on whether the distribution rule had already been used in a previous period.

for further information see note 3066714

entry.title: PDI BADI for Exclusion of Withholding Tax Items
entry.content:
Dear all,

 

does anybody know how this new feature works, its purpose and way of utilizing it?

The only explanation I found was this:

You can call Partner Development Infrastructure (PDI) - Business Add-Ins (BADI) in to SAP application studio to build reusable Add-Ins. Using PDI-BADI, you can now customize your logic and exclude the Invoices (Payment and Clearing documents) that are not required for Tax Reporting. The split invoice documents for Tax Reporting are sent back to SAP, and the withholding tax returns (856, 857,102) are posted based on the documents received.

Thanks

LM

entry.title: New Community Package: A Cloud Integration Alternative for SAP Process Orchestration Adapter Modules
entry.content:
We’re excited to introduce a new Community Content integration package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, specifically designed to streamline migrating from SAP Process Orchestration (PO) to SAP Cloud Integration.

This package brings scripts and integration flows to work as alternatives for the already known Adapter Modules that exist today on SAP Process Integration/Orchestration, such as transforming messages payloads from XML to CSV or Plain Text (e.g., fixed length, delimited) and vice-versa.

Currently, the package includes a collection of scripts that can be used as alternatives to the following SAP Process Orchestration Adapter Modules:

  • MessageTransformBean
  • XMLAnonymizer

Additionally, the package features sample Integration Flows for various use cases, along with detailed configuration guides for each script. These resources will help you understand how to effectively integrate the scripts into your scenarios.

In summary, the approach involves adding a Content Modifier before the Groovy Script step to set up properties used as configuration inputs for the script. The property names are mostly aligned with those in SAP Process Orchestration, making the transition easier for those familiar with the existing modules.

Looking ahead, we plan to extend this package with additional Groovy scripts and integration flow alternatives for other modules, such as:

  • IDOCFlatToXmlConvertor
  • IDOCXmlToFlatConvertor
  • AddSOAPHeaderBean
  • PayloadSwapBean

Whether you are migrating existing SAP Process Integration/Orchestration scenarios or building new ones directly on Cloud Integration, this package offers valuable support and enables you to take advantage of modernized approaches to transformation.

Access it now on Business Accelerator Hub and feel free to share feedback: Overview | Message Transformation Utilities | SAP Business Accelerator Hub

 

Please note that a community package is provided as a standalone component under the Apache License, v. 2.0 and, is not part of any SAP product. SAP does not support Community Content. Please raise any issues at the associated GitHub project.

You can find the official release plans for SAP product development on SAP Roadmap Explorer. Here you can find features of this package and when they will be released as a standard feature in the SAP Integration Suite.

If one of these features is required in the SAP Integration Suite in a timely manner but not listed in the SAP Roadmap Explore, we therefore ask you to create a “Feature Improvement” on SAP Customer Influence – Campaign: SAP Integration Suite or vote for it, if exactly same “Feature Improvement” is already available. All “Feature Improvements” and especially their votes have a significant influence on the prioritization of the features to be delivered in the SAP Integration Suite.

Hi folks,

I've recorded a demo on how to create your SAP Universal ID, "purchase" a SAP Certification exam attempt using a campaign code and schedule your SAP Certification exam in one flow.

Some tips:

  • Be sure to select your country before attempting to add any item to your shopping basket on training.sap.com 
  • Have a secondary email address ready to connect with your SAP Universal ID
  • Consult our troubleshooting guide if you have any issues with the campaign code process
  • Contact sapcertification@questionmark.com if there are problems with your exam scheduling or experience

Watch the demo:

Timestamps:

1:11 - Use campaign code to book a free SAP Certification Exam attempt
3:22 - Link additional email to your UID
5:48 - Schedule your exam
10:44 - Cancel your appointment

Here is the single SAP Certification attempt product to check out with. This attempt applies to all SAP exams. There is no need to look for a specific exam title until later in the process.

Best of luck with your exam!

-David

entry.title: Re: Fiori Currency Conversion Issue
entry.content:
Have you created a custom currency code type definition in table TCURC ?  E.g. using the configuration menu in transaction SPRO. 

Manipulating decimal places on the front-end only in this way is very risky. When you change the decimal places you change the meaning of the value. That affects everywhere that value is used, e.g. when converting from local to foreign currency. 

Retail customers often use 4 decimal place currencies like this - and for that you need a custom currency code type definition. Then all the currency conversions will work reliably system wide. 

From what I understand of your approach this is really ABAP fundamentals and actually nothing to do with SAP Fiori - SAP Fiori is applying the conversion correctly (its actually just reflecting what the ABAP currency conversion is automatically doing via the CDS View) . You might want to ask your question using the ABAP tag.

entry.content:
Hi.
Customer can create some CDS view in Fiori without me, but it will be view with OData2 service. As I learned, I cannot create OData4 service in Fiori.
I must use ADT(Eclipse) or BTP Cockpit->"SAP Business Application Studio". Is it full list of tools for OData4 service creating?
Also I must develop my view and service in DEV tenant. Then I can put my view and sevice into collection and push it to my TEST tenant.
Then to my PROD tenant.
Doc: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a630d57fc5004c6383e7a81efee7a8bb/aa60b129af7b4ce8ae052618c8315d29.html

The question is what mechanism should I use if I want to deliver my collection to customers? Not to my own production tenant.

entry.title: Re: iphone or ipad with eSIM have no device ID, how to se...
entry.content:
Dear PhoebeYeo

Without device id, old mobile app could not be used.

However you could still use SAP Business One sales (note 2267084) and service app(note 2639956)

Thank You.

Kind Regards
Nidhi Singh

entry.title: AES Encryption - without IV
entry.content:
Hi,

We are trying to encrypt a text with a secret key - 'without passing the IV'. Referring to https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-blog-posts/aes-encryption-in-abap/ba-p/13401465

we were able to arrive at the logic along with the IV, however without the IV the correct encoding doesnt take place.  

Any pointers on how we can achieve this will be helpful.

entry.title: Re: Datasphere - Last Logon Time
entry.content: Yes, you would need to create a DB user for each user. For now there is no statistics like in BW.
